    Hooking Up Agents To Your Backlog Solution

    Let's run ./.sandcastle/main.ts to see what happens. The agent spins up and we can watch it in action.

    The GitHub issue list state returns just 37 tokens from a tiny little description. The agent kicks off by exploring the repo structure and understanding the codebase before trying to fix the issue.

    Understanding Token Permissions

    One question that people often have with this exercise is: why would you take your GitHub token and give it to an agent?

    The key is that you can create really sharp tokens that only allow it to do certain things. For instance, here you might want it to:

    • Read issues
    • Comment on issues
    • Close issues

    You don't really even want it to be able to create its own issues. GitHub allows you to create a really fine-grained access token that only allows your agent to do certain things. That's how you'd implement it safely.

    Finding and Fixing the Bug

    Scrolling down to the bottom, the agent has found the correct dev UI component and is looking at the exact code and the API route. It seems to be on the right track.

    The agent has identified the bug: when a user is selected, the form submits via React Router's client-side navigation. The open state stays true and the dropdown never closes.

    The Commit

    There's code being changed and it's already been committed. The commit has succeeded with all pre-commit hooks passing. The agent has appended no more tasks and used the 30K context window efficiently.

    Verification in GitHub

    Going to the GitHub issues, there's now zero open and one closed. Looking at the closed issue, it's the one the agent just fixed. The agent added a comment explaining that it closes immediately when a user was selected. We get an element of observability here - we understand why the issue was closed because the agent has told us.

    Testing the Fix

    Now for the moment of truth. Going to the dev UI panel, switching the user to Alex Rivera, and it closes. The power of AI in action.

    We've hooked up the agent to the issue tracker and it's able to churn through backlog items automatically.

    Extending the Exercise

    If you want to extend this exercise, you can:

    • Go into the application
    • Run QA on a bunch of stuff
    • Find a bunch of issues and report them as GitHub issues
    • Run the AFK agent again and see how it does

    Overall, we've proven that the concept works. The agent can autonomously identify bugs, commit fixes, and close issues with proper documentation.
